planting.note: In mild-winter climates (zones 8–10) plant corms in fall for late-winter/spring bloom; in cold climates start indoors and transplant after last frost.

directions: Soak corms in cool water for 4 hours before planting, then set them claw-side down about 2 in deep in well-drained, fertile soil. Ranunculus thrives in cool temperatures (45–65°F) and will decline when heat arrives, so timing is critical. In zones 9–10 plant October through January for blooms from February through May; provide afternoon shade in the warmest areas. After foliage yellows, allow corms to dry in situ or lift, cure, and store in a cool dry location for replanting.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in mild climates (zones 7–10) or in early spring after danger of hard frost has passed in colder zones.

directions: Soak the small, irregular corms in water for 4–6 hours before planting; set them about 2 in deep with the pointed side down (or on their side if unsure). Anemones prefer cool, well-drained soil and perform best where winters are mild and springs are long and cool. Poppy-like blooms in vivid red, blue, purple, white, and bicolors appear over a 6–8 week period in late winter to spring. In cold climates treat as an annual, planting corms in spring for early summer color.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in frost-free areas (zones 9–10) or start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost in cold climates.

directions: Plant corms 2 in deep and 2–3 in apart in gritty, well-drained soil; freesias resent wet conditions especially after bloom. The fragrant, funnel-shaped flowers on arching stems need staking or a grow-through support as stems are slender. In frost-free gardens the corms are perennial and naturalize freely; in colder climates lift after foliage dies, dry thoroughly, and store in a cool dry place. Corms multiply quickly — divide every 2–3 years to maintain bloom quality.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring around last frost; in zones 6–7 apply a thick mulch in fall to protect overwintering corms.

directions: Plant corms 3–4 in deep in groups of 6 or more for the best visual impact; crocosmia forms clumps through underground stolons. Arching sprays of scarlet, orange, or yellow tubular flowers appear from midsummer into fall and are excellent for cutting. Where hardy, divide congested clumps every 3–4 years in early spring to refresh bloom; in zone 5 and colder, lift and store over winter like gladiolus. 'Lucifer' is among the hardiest cultivars, tolerating zone 5 with reliable mulching.

planting.note: Plant corms after last frost in spring for summer bloom; in mild-winter climates (zones 9–10) plant in fall.

directions: Plant corms 3–4 in deep in fertile, well-drained soil; watsonia closely resembles gladiolus and thrives in similar conditions. The tall, sword-like foliage produces elegant spikes of tubular flowers in shades of pink, red, coral, white, and bicolor throughout summer. In frost-free climates corms multiply into large colonies and can become invasive — divide every few years to manage spread. In colder zones lift corms after frost kills the foliage, cure, and store in a cool, dry, frost-free location.

planting.note: Plant bulbs after all frost danger passes in spring, once soil has warmed above 60°F.

directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 in deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; each exotic three-petaled flower lasts only one day but new buds open in succession over weeks. The vivid blooms come in yellow, orange, red, pink, and white, typically with a heavily spotted or blotched center cup. Plant in groups of a dozen or more for a showy display from midsummer into fall. In zones colder than 8, dig bulbs after first frost, dry thoroughly, and store in paper bags in a cool, frost-free location.

planting.note: Plant tubers horizontally 4 in deep after last frost; in zones 8+ can be planted in early spring once soil is 65°F.

directions: Plant the fragile, finger-shaped tubers horizontally 4 in deep, handling carefully to avoid snapping them; provide a trellis or support as the vine climbs by leaf-tip tendrils. The striking reflexed flowers in flame-red and yellow with wavy-edged petals are produced freely from midsummer into fall. All parts of the plant are highly toxic if ingested — site away from children and pets. In zones below 8, dig tubers after foliage dies, dry them, and store in dry peat moss at 50–60°F over winter.

planting.note: Plant tubers after all frost danger passes and soil is reliably above 70°F; cold soil causes rot.

directions: Set tubers knobby-side up, 2 in deep in rich, moist, well-drained soil; for a head start, pot tubers indoors 6–8 weeks before the last frost date in a warm (75–80°F) location. Caladiums are prized for spectacular heart-shaped foliage in combinations of red, pink, white, and green rather than for their flowers. They excel in shady spots where little else provides vibrant color and thrive in humid conditions. In fall, before temperatures drop below 50°F, dig tubers, allow to cure for 2 weeks, and store in dry vermiculite at 60–65°F.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or early spring as soon as the ground can be worked; fall planting is preferred in zones 4–7.

directions: Plant bulbs 6–8 in deep (3 times the bulb diameter) in well-drained, organically rich, slightly acidic soil; apply mulch to keep roots cool. Oriental lilies prefer to have their heads in the sun and their feet in the shade — interplant with low perennials to shade the root zone. The large, intensely fragrant blooms appear in mid- to late summer; deadhead after flowering but allow foliage to remain until it yellows naturally. Avoid overhead watering to reduce the risk of botrytis; stake tall cultivars against wind damage.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall at least 6 weeks before the ground freezes; disturb as little as possible once established.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 in deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil; martagon lilies are unusual in tolerating partial shade and actually prefer dappled woodland conditions. Whorled leaves and pendant, turks-cap-shaped flowers in pink, purple, or white with recurved petals appear in early summer. Martagon lilies are slow to establish and may not bloom for 2 years after planting — once settled they are very long-lived and resent being disturbed. Self-seeding is common; seedlings take 4–7 years to bloom.

planting.note: Plant the spider-like fleshy roots in fall, spreading them horizontally 4 in deep in a well-drained, sunny site.

directions: Spread the octopus-like fleshy roots horizontally over a mound of gritty soil, crown just below the surface, with perfect drainage — wet winter soil rots the crowns quickly. The strap-like foliage emerges in early spring and can be killed by a late frost; a light cover of straw protects emerging growth in zones 5–6. Spectacular bottle-brush spikes up to 8 ft tall erupt in late spring to early summer in shades of white, yellow, orange, and pink. Leaves die back after bloom, so interplant with later-emerging perennials to fill the gap.

planting.note: Plant large bulbs in fall at a depth of 3–4 times the bulb diameter (typically 6–8 in) in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall, pointed side up, at 3–4 times their diameter in depth; good drainage is essential as bulbs rot in waterlogged soil. The bold, softball-sized purple globes atop tall stems create dramatic vertical accents in late spring to early summer borders. Foliage, which smells of onion when crushed, often begins to yellow before flowers are fully open — conceal the base with later-rising perennials. Allow seedheads to remain for architectural interest; deadhead if you wish to prevent excessive self-seeding.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–6 in deep in moist, fertile soil; camassia tolerates seasonally wet conditions better than most bulbs.

directions: Plant bulbs 4–6 in deep in organically rich, moisture-retentive soil; unlike many spring bulbs, camassia tolerates and even prefers seasonally moist or wet ground. Star-shaped flowers in blue, violet, or creamy white appear on tall spikes in late spring, bridging the gap between early bulbs and summer perennials. Allow foliage to die back naturally after blooming; bulbs naturalize readily in meadow or prairie-style plantings. Deer and rodents avoid camassia bulbs.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 3 in deep in fall; naturalize in lawns, rock gardens, or under deciduous trees.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall, 3 in deep and 2–3 in apart, in any well-drained soil; glory-of-the-snow blooms very early, often pushing through snow in late winter to early spring. The vivid blue, star-shaped flowers with white centers naturalize freely by both offsets and self-seeding, forming large colonies over time. Plant in large drifts under deciduous trees where they receive full sun while leafless and will not be disturbed during summer dormancy. No special care is needed once established; bulbs rarely need dividing.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs in fall, 3 in deep; alternatively plant 'in the green' (just after bloom) for better establishment.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 in deep in moisture-retentive, humus-rich soil under deciduous trees or in a shaded border; snowdrops are one of the first bulbs to bloom, often in January or February. They establish most successfully when transplanted 'in the green' — lifted and divided just after blooming while foliage is still active. Clumps multiply steadily and benefit from division every 3–4 years right after flowering to prevent overcrowding. Allow foliage to die back naturally; avoid mowing naturalised areas until leaves have fully yellowed.

planting.note: Plant tubers in fall as soon as available — they dry out quickly; soak in water for 24 hours before planting.

directions: Soak the small, hard tubers in water for 24 hours before planting to rehydrate them; plant 2 in deep under deciduous trees or shrubs as soon as received in late summer or early fall. Bright yellow cup-shaped flowers surrounded by a green ruff appear in late winter, often alongside snowdrops, before any leaves emerge on overhead trees. Winter aconite self-seeds freely and slowly forms large, cheerful carpets if left undisturbed. Best planted where they can stay permanently; they strongly dislike being moved.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in early fall, 4 in deep, in moist but well-drained soil; ideal for naturalizing in damp meadows.

directions: Plant bulbs in early fall, 4 in deep and 3–4 in apart, in organically rich, moisture-retentive but not waterlogged soil; perfect for damp meadow or pond-edge plantings. The nodding, checkerboard-patterned bells in deep purple, white, or pink appear in mid-spring and are among the most intricately patterned flowers in the spring garden. Naturalizes freely in damp grassland — avoid mowing until foliage has died back completely in early summer. Once established the bulbs multiply steadily; divide congested clumps in early fall.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall, 3–4 in deep; foliage emerges in fall and overwinters, so plant early enough for roots to establish.

directions: Plant small bulbs 3–4 in deep in any well-drained soil; foliage emerges in fall and overwinters as a low ground cover, with tight clusters of cobalt-blue, urn-shaped flowers appearing in early to mid-spring. Muscari naturalizes aggressively by offsets and self-seeding and can spread widely — site where this is desirable or divide clumps every few years in fall to control spread. Excellent planted beneath tulips and daffodils to create complementary color combinations. Deer and rodents generally leave them alone.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall, 4–6 in deep; in zones 5–6 apply a winter mulch for protection.

directions: Plant bulbs 4–6 in deep in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Dutch iris bloom in late spring to early summer and are superb cut flowers with a vase life of 7–10 days. The large falls and standards come in shades of blue, purple, yellow, white, and bicolor. After bloom, allow foliage to die back naturally; in warm, humid climates the bulbs may rot in summer — lift and store dry in a cool place if this occurs. Replant in early fall and incorporate grit into heavy clay soils to improve drainage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring as growth begins, or in late summer after flowering; set rhizomes just below soil surface.

directions: Plant rhizomes just 1–2 in below the soil surface in moisture-retentive, slightly acidic soil; unlike bearded iris, Siberian iris prefers consistently moist conditions and tolerates wet spots. Graceful, narrow foliage forms attractive clumps all season, topped by elegant blue, purple, or white flowers in early summer. Siberian iris are very long-lived and rarely need dividing — only do so when the center of the clump dies out or blooming diminishes, typically every 5–8 years. Division is best done immediately after flowering or in early fall; replant promptly and water well.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost or immediately after late-summer bloom; keep roots consistently moist after planting.

directions: Plant rhizomes 2 in deep in rich, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), moisture-retentive to wet soil; Japanese iris excel at pond margins and rain gardens. The enormous flat flowers up to 10 in across appear in early to midsummer in shades of purple, blue, white, and lavender, often with intricate veining. Avoid lime or wood ash near these plants — alkaline soil causes yellowing and poor growth. Divide every 3–4 years in late summer or early spring, replanting healthy outer fans and discarding the woody centers.

planting.note: Direct sow around last frost date; seeds need cool temperatures to germinate and benefit from cold stratification.

directions: Refrigerate seeds for one week before sowing; then direct sow on the soil surface in early spring since seeds need light and cool temperatures to germinate. Thin to 9–12 in apart — crowded plants are prone to powdery mildew. The tall, architectural stems bear small white flowers nestled inside large, shell-shaped green calyces that remain ornamental for months and dry beautifully. Cut stems for fresh or dried arrangements before calyces begin to brown.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost, or sow in fall where winters are mild.

directions: Sow seeds on the soil surface in early spring as they need light and cool temperatures to germinate; do not cover seeds. Orlaya produces flat-topped umbel flowers with large, irregular outer florets that create a delicate lace effect in pure white, ideal as a cut flower filler. It prefers lean, well-drained soil — overly rich conditions produce lush foliage at the expense of flowers. Plants self-sow readily where happy, returning reliably if the ground is not disturbed; collect seed or allow them to fall for the following year.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost, or sow in fall in zones 7+ for overwintering.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in cool soil; germination is rapid in the 55–65°F range and slows dramatically in warm conditions. The blue-green waxy foliage and pendant, tubular flowers in deep violet-purple with showy blue-purple bracts are a magnet for bumblebees. Cerinthe prefers cool weather and will bolt and die in summer heat; in mild climates it self-sows prolifically and may return year after year. Harvest stems when bracts are fully colored for a distinctive and long-lasting cut flower.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; does not transplant well — sow in situ.

directions: Sow seeds directly where they are to grow, pressing them onto moist soil — they need light to germinate; thin to 9–12 in apart. Ammi majus produces flat, lacy white umbels similar to Queen Anne's Lace but with cleaner, more refined flowers that are highly valued by florists. The feathery foliage and long stems make it an ideal filler in bouquets with a vase life of 7–10 days. Successive sowings 3–4 weeks apart extend the harvest period; plants bolt in summer heat so succession-sow in spring only.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; can also sow in fall in mild winter climates.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 in deep in early spring directly where they are to grow; cover seeds lightly as they germinate better in darkness. The clusters of tiny, intensely blue flowers resemble true forget-me-nots but bloom for a much longer period — from late spring well into summer. Shear plants back by one-third after the first main flush to encourage a second wave of bloom. Plants self-sow freely; allow some seeds to drop for naturalised succession planting in subsequent years.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after last frost into a dry, sunny site.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant to a sunny, very well-drained site — catananche is native to dry Mediterranean hillsides and rots in moist soil. The papery, cornflower-blue blooms on wiry stems are excellent cut or dried flowers that hold their color well. Shear plants by half after the first flush in early summer to encourage repeat flowering into fall. Best treated as a short-lived perennial; replace plants every 2–3 years or allow to self-sow for a continuing colony.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; tap-rooted and resents transplanting — sow where plants will remain.

directions: Sow seeds directly in well-drained, even poor, sandy or gravelly soil; sea holly develops a deep taproot and strongly dislikes being transplanted after establishment. The metallic steel-blue stems, bracts, and thimble-shaped flower heads appear in midsummer and hold their color and structure well into fall. Drought-tolerant once established, sea holly declines in rich, moist soil — lean, infertile conditions produce the best blue coloration. Cut stems for fresh or dried arrangements before flowers fully open to preserve the vivid blue color.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring 2–4 weeks before last frost; does not transplant well due to taproot.

directions: Sow seeds directly in a sunny, well-drained location in early spring; thin to 9–12 in apart as seedlings emerge. The fragrant, fringed, powder-puff flowers in shades of pink, purple, yellow, and white are excellent for cutting, with a pleasant, musky sweet scent. Plants prefer cool weather and will begin to decline as summer heat intensifies — make a second sowing in late summer for fall bloom in warm climates. Successive sowings every 3–4 weeks in spring extend the flowering season through early summer.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; prefers to be direct sown as transplants disturb its taproot.

directions: Direct sow after last frost in full sun with lean, well-drained soil; xeranthemum is one of the easiest everlastings to grow and tolerates poor, dry conditions well. The papery, daisy-like flowers in shades of lavender, pink, purple, and white are naturally self-preserving and air-dry on the stem. Harvest stems for drying when flowers are about two-thirds open; hang upside down in a warm, airy location for 2–3 weeks. Successive sowings 2–3 weeks apart can extend the harvest season into early fall.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or in summer for flowers the following spring; behaves as a biennial in most climates.

directions: Sow seeds in spring or early summer; plants develop as a rosette the first year and flower the following spring — a true biennial. Purple or white cross-shaped flowers appear in mid-spring, followed by the flat, papery-silver seed pods that give the plant its common names 'honesty' and 'money plant.' To harvest pods for dried arrangements, cut when the outer husks have dried and carefully peel away both sides to reveal the lustrous translucent membrane. Plants self-sow freely and once established will perpetuate themselves year after year.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger has passed into a sunny, alkaline-friendly site.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost, sowing 1/4 in deep in a light germination mix; transplant outdoors after frost into well-drained, slightly alkaline or neutral soil. The domed, pincushion flowers in rich maroon, lavender, white, pink, and burgundy are magnets for butterflies and superb for cutting. Deadhead consistently to prevent self-seeding and extend the long bloom period from early summer through frost. Avoid rich, wet soils — scabiosa thrives in moderately lean conditions and is drought-tolerant once established.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a shaded, moist site; or plant divisions/transplants in early spring.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist, shaded to partly shaded woodland soil; Virginia bluebells are a spring ephemeral that goes dormant by early summer, disappearing completely underground. The clusters of pink buds open to sky-blue, nodding tubular flowers in early to mid-spring and are among the most beautiful of native wildflowers. Mark the location of clumps so they are not accidentally disturbed; interplant with hostas, ferns, or other perennials that will fill the gap when bluebells go dormant. Divide crowded clumps or transplant in early fall when dormant.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost with a cold stratification period; or plant divisions in spring or fall.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist, well-drained shade; foamflower spreads by stolons to form a weed-suppressing ground cover with attractive, deeply lobed, often patterned foliage. Frothy spikes of tiny white to pink flowers appear in mid-spring above the foliage and last for 4–6 weeks. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in early spring to prevent overcrowding and maintain vigor. Many named cultivars with exceptional leaf markings are available and are best propagated by division to maintain cultivar characteristics.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; surface sow as seeds need light to germinate — do not cover.

directions: Press seeds onto the surface of a moist germination medium — do not cover, as light is required for germination; transplant to a humus-rich, well-drained site after last frost. Grown primarily for their spectacular mound-forming foliage in shades of burgundy, copper, lime, silver, and purple, heucheras also produce airy wands of tiny flowers beloved by hummingbirds. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to rejuvenate the woody crowns; reset divisions with the crown at soil level. Most modern hybrids tolerate more sun with adequate moisture but prefer afternoon shade in hot climates.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in a shaded, moist site; division is the most reliable propagation method.

directions: Plant in deep shade to partial shade in humus-rich, consistently moist soil; lungwort is one of the best shade perennials, offering both attractive silver-spotted foliage and early spring flowers. Pink buds open to blue or violet tubular flowers in early spring, often when little else is in bloom, providing nectar for early pollinators. In hot, dry summers the leaves may develop powdery mildew — shear back affected foliage after flowering and new, fresh leaves will emerge. Divide clumps every 3–4 years immediately after flowering to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring as soon as plants can be purchased; also self-seeds moderately in moist, shaded sites.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in cool, humus-rich, moist but well-drained soil; brunnera is one of the best foliage plants for shade, particularly the silver-variegated cultivars like 'Jack Frost.' Delicate sprays of forget-me-not blue flowers appear in early to mid-spring above the large, heart-shaped leaves. In hot or dry conditions the leaves may scorch — consistent moisture and afternoon shade are essential in warmer climates. Divide clumps every 4–5 years in early spring; variegated cultivars must be divided rather than grown from seed to maintain their markings.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost with cold stratification; or plant divisions in early spring.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist soil in partial shade; tall meadow rue creates an airy, vertical accent with blue-green, columbine-like foliage and billowing clouds of lavender-purple flowers in midsummer. The delicate flowers are held on strong, self-supporting stems and sway gracefully in a breeze. Seldom needs staking if grown in adequate light; divide every 4–5 years in early spring to keep plants vigorous. Excellent in a woodland border or at the back of a shaded perennial bed.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring as growth begins; they establish quickly and bloom in their first season from divisions.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, consistently moist, well-drained shade; toad lily is an elegant late-season bloomer that produces exotic-looking, orchid-like flowers in white, purple, or lilac with dark spots from late summer through fall. The arching stems with clasping leaves create a graceful, architectural presence throughout the season. Consistent moisture is essential — plants decline in dry soil; mulch heavily to maintain soil moisture and temperature. Divide every 3–4 years in early spring to manage the slowly spreading clumps.

planting.note: Plant tuber-like roots in fall or early spring in a cool, moist, partially shaded site; wear gloves — all parts are highly toxic.

directions: Plant with the tuber-like roots 3–4 in deep in rich, cool, moist, well-drained soil; monkshood is a stately perennial with bold, deeply lobed foliage and tall spikes of deep blue-violet helmet-shaped flowers in late summer to early fall. All parts of the plant are extremely toxic if ingested or if the sap contacts broken skin — always wear gloves when handling and wash hands thoroughly after. It is one of the best late-season blues in the perennial garden and thrives where summers are cool. Divide every 4–5 years in early spring; keep plants where they will not be disturbed by children or pets.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring; slow to establish but eventually spreads vigorously.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, moisture-retentive, well-drained soil in partial shade; Japanese anemone is slow to establish in its first 1–2 years but spreads freely by underground runners once settled. Single or semi-double flowers in white, pink, or deep rose appear on tall, branched stems from late summer through fall when few other perennials are in bloom. Can become invasive in rich, moist conditions — site carefully or contain with buried barriers. Divide in early spring every 3–4 years to control spread or increase the planting.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; or start indoors in late winter with 8–10 weeks of cold moist stratification.

directions: Sow seeds in fall and allow winter cold to naturally stratify them, or cold-stratify indoors for 8–10 weeks before spring sowing; plant in lean, well-drained to dry soil in full sun. The nodding, burgundy-pink flowers in mid-spring are followed by the spectacular feathery, smoke-like seed plumes that give the plant its name and provide ornamental interest for weeks. Prairie smoke is a rugged, drought-tolerant native wildflower that thrives in rocky or sandy soils where more demanding plants struggle. It spreads slowly into a weed-suppressing groundcover in open, sunny areas.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall; sap and foliage can irritate skin — handle with gloves and avoid contact with eyes.

directions: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a sunny, gravelly, or rocky, very well-drained site; pasque flower germinates best from fresh seed with natural cold stratification and is slow to establish but extremely long-lived once settled. Silky, nodding purple, lavender, or white flowers emerge in early spring from the fuzzy, hairy foliage and are followed by fluffy, ornamental seedheads similar to clematis. Perfectly drained, lean, alkaline or neutral soil is essential — clay or continuously wet conditions are fatal. Resents transplanting; sow in situ or plant container-grown specimens when small.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; clump-forming cultivars are better-behaved than the straight species.

directions: Plant in full sun in average to poor, well-drained soil; goldenrod thrives in lean conditions and becomes overly lax and floppy in rich, moist soil. The arching plumes of golden-yellow flowers from late summer through fall are essential late-season food for monarch butterflies and dozens of native bee species. Contrary to popular belief, goldenrod does not cause hay fever — its pollen is heavy and insect-carried, not windborne. Choose named compact cultivars like 'Fireworks' or 'Little Lemon' for well-behaved garden varieties; divide every 2–3 years to control spread.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; cold stratification for 4–6 weeks improves germination rates.

directions: Sow seeds indoors after cold stratification or plant divisions in spring in moist to moderately moist, organically rich soil; culver's root is an imposing native prairie plant with whorled, lance-shaped leaves and tall candelabra-like spikes of white to pale lavender flowers in mid- to late summer. The architectural form makes it a striking back-of-border plant, and the flowers attract a wide variety of native bees and butterflies. Rarely needs staking when grown in adequate light; divide every 4–5 years in spring to maintain vigor. Excellent companion for joe-pye weed, prairie blazing star, and native grasses.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall for natural cold stratification; transplants poorly due to deep taproot.

directions: Sow fresh seed in fall directly in a sunny prairie or meadow garden in lean, well-drained soil; rattlesnake master develops a deep taproot and resents being moved after establishment. The dramatic, yucca-like basal rosette of spiny blue-green leaves is topped by tall branched stems bearing small, spiny, globe-shaped white flower heads in midsummer that are highly attractive to native bees. It is extremely drought-tolerant and actually performs better in poor, sandy or rocky soil than in rich garden beds. The sculptural seedheads provide winter interest and are prized for dried arrangements.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification; alternatively cold-stratify indoors and spring-sow after last frost.

directions: Sow in lean to average, well-drained soil in full sun; wild quinine is a sturdy native prairie plant that tolerates heat, drought, and clay once established. The flat-topped clusters of small white button flowers appear in midsummer and persist for many weeks, drawing in a wide diversity of native bees, wasps, and beetles. The button-like, persistent seed heads provide architectural interest into fall and winter. A reliable, long-lived plant that requires virtually no maintenance once established in a sunny meadow or native plant garden.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; transplant after soil has warmed thoroughly.

directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost at 70–75°F; transplant after frost danger passes into full sun and fertile, well-drained soil — warm soil is essential for strong establishment. Grown as an annual for its striking deep burgundy-purple foliage and the large, cylindrical flower/grain heads that arch gracefully under their own weight in late summer. An excellent anchor plant in tropical-style or mixed annual borders, and the dried seed heads are useful in arrangements. Highly attractive to birds in fall — leave some plants intact for wildlife value.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants after last frost; this cultivar does not come true from seed and is propagated by division.

directions: Plant after last frost in full sun and very well-drained soil; purple fountain grass is grown as an annual north of zone 9 for its arching burgundy-red foliage and showy, rose-tinged plumes from midsummer through frost. It thrives in heat and drought once established but requires consistent moisture the first few weeks after transplanting. In frost-free climates it is a perennial that can become invasive — avoid planting near natural areas and remove flower heads before seeds ripen. Bring container-grown specimens indoors before frost to overwinter as a houseplant in colder zones.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; division every 2–3 years maintains the intense blue color.

directions: Plant in full sun and very well-drained, lean to average soil; blue fescue is a cool-season grass that goes semi-dormant in the heat of summer and performs best in cool, dry climates. The tight, spiky mounds of intensely silvery-blue foliage are striking as edging or mass plantings; tan flower spikes appear in early summer. Divide every 2–3 years in early spring, discarding the dead center and replanting the vigorous outer portions — this maintains the best blue coloration. Avoid heavy, wet clay soils and humid conditions which promote crown rot.

planting.note: Direct sow around last frost date in a sunny, dry site; self-seeds profusely — site away from natural areas.

directions: Sow seeds directly in full sun in lean, very well-drained soil — rich or moist conditions produce weak, floppy plants. The hair-fine, bright green leaves form airy, billowing mounds that catch every breeze, creating a constant motion in the landscape; the foliage turns blonde and golden in summer heat. This grass self-seeds very aggressively and is considered invasive in California and some southwestern states — deadhead before seeds ripen if growing in areas with native grasslands. Cut back hard in late winter before new growth begins.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring when soil has warmed; miscanthus establishes best in warm soil and can be slow in cold springs.

directions: Plant divisions in full sun in any well-drained to moderately moist soil; maiden grass forms large, dramatic clumps with arching, green or variegated foliage and showy, feathery silver plumes in late summer and fall. Cut back hard in late winter or early spring before new growth emerges — use pruners or a chainsaw on large clumps; tie the clump with twine before cutting to simplify cleanup. Divide every 3–5 years in spring when the center of the clump dies out. Some cultivars are sterile; check before planting, as seeding types can naturalize aggressively in some regions.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring; avoid fall planting in zones 6–7 as plants need a full season to establish before winter.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained, lean to average soil; muhly grass is tolerant of sandy soil, heat, humidity, and drought once established and is an outstanding choice for hot, dry southern landscapes. The main attraction is the spectacular cloud of airy, rose-pink flower plumes in September–October that transform the planting into something otherworldly when backlit by low autumn sun. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter before new growth begins; avoid heavy organic mulching which can cause crown rot in humid climates. Divide every 4–5 years in spring to rejuvenate large clumps.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; 'Karl Foerster' is sterile and must be propagated by division.

directions: Plant in full sun to light shade in average, moisture-retentive soil; 'Karl Foerster' feather reed grass is one of the most reliable and widely adapted ornamental grasses, tolerating clay soil, humidity, and cold winters that defeat many others. Strongly upright, narrow clumps emerge early in spring with feathery bronze-pink plumes appearing in early summer that fade to wheat-gold and persist through winter. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter or early spring before new growth begins. Divide every 4–5 years in spring when the center begins to die out.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in spring, or plant divisions in spring in full sun and lean, well-drained soil.

directions: Direct sow in full sun in lean, well-drained to dry soil; little bluestem is a native prairie grass of exceptional ornamental value that thrives in poor, sandy, or rocky soil where it develops the best fall color. Blue-green summer foliage turns brilliant coppery-orange and burgundy-red in fall, and the silver-white fluffy seedheads persist through winter, capturing hoarfrost beautifully. In rich, moist soil plants become floppy and lose their characteristic upright form. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter before new growth emerges; divide every 3–4 years if needed.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in spring; warm soil (above 60°F) promotes best germination.

directions: Sow seeds in full sun after last frost in average to poor, well-drained soil; switchgrass is extremely adaptable and tolerates clay, sand, wet or dry conditions, and even brief flooding — a true workhorse native grass. The airy, branching panicles of tiny seeds in late summer create a misty, luminous effect, and many cultivars develop spectacular red, burgundy, or orange fall color. Cut back to 4–6 in in late winter; divide every 3–4 years in spring when the clump center dies out. Named cultivars like 'Shenandoah' and 'Prairie Fire' offer predictably strong red fall color.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; very slow to establish from seed — divisions or plugs reach ornamental size much faster.

directions: Sow seeds in full sun in lean, well-drained, sandy or rocky soil after last frost; prairie dropseed is a slow-growing native that takes 2–3 years from seed to develop into an ornamental clump. The extremely fine-textured, emerald-green foliage forms a perfect arching fountain shape, and open, airy flower panicles in late summer emit a distinctive fragrance of warm popcorn or cilantro. Fall color is a beautiful tawny orange-gold and the foliage persists attractively through winter. Once established it is extremely drought- and heat-tolerant and virtually maintenance-free.
